content.text: AWS imposes numerical limits on almost every resource and API operation in every service. These limits — called service quotas (previously called service limits) — define the maximum number of a resource you can create, or the maximum rate at which you can call an API, within a single AWS account in a single AWS Region.

Quotas exist for two reasons. First, they protect AWS infrastructure: a runaway workload in one account cannot consume all available capacity in a shared data center, which would impact other customers. Second, they protect you: hitting a quota is far better than receiving a surprise bill for thousands of unexpected resources. Quotas are the circuit breakers of the cloud.

Unlike physical servers where capacity is visibly finite, cloud resources feel unlimited until you hit a quota. Understanding quota mechanics before you need them is essential for production readiness — quota increase requests can take up to several business days for services that require AWS review.

attrs.alt: AWS Service Quotas — types, scope, and lifecycle management

content.text: The diagram above shows the two categories of quotas and the five-step management lifecycle. Every quota falls into one of two categories: adjustable quotas (which AWS will raise on request) and non-adjustable quotas (which are architectural or technical hard limits that cannot change regardless of business justification).

Adjustable quotas represent the majority of limits you will encounter in practice. They exist because AWS has provisioned a safe default, not because the underlying infrastructure is truly limited to that number. Most teams hit adjustable quotas during scale-out events, and AWS routinely approves increases for legitimate production use cases.

Non-adjustable quotas reflect true technical constraints — an IAM policy document cannot exceed 6,144 characters because the IAM evaluation engine imposes that constraint on every API call. An S3 object cannot exceed 5 TB because that is the maximum multipart upload size the protocol supports. These limits cannot be raised.

Every quota applies to a specific (account, region) pair. Your EC2 vCPU quota in us-east-1 is entirely independent of your quota in eu-west-1. This has an important implication: if you expand your deployment to a new region, you must proactively request quota increases there too — the increases you obtained in your primary region do not transfer.
Running-On-Demand Standard (A, C, D, H, I, M, R, T, Z) instances: 32 vCPUs default → you may need 500+ for production
Lambda concurrent executions: 1,000 per region default → high-traffic APIs often need 3,000–10,000
RDS DB instances: 40 per region default → large environments with many microservices need 100+
VPCs per region: 5 default → most organizations request 50+ for environment isolation

Most quotas live at the service level — your 'EC2 vCPU limit' is an EC2 service quota. A handful of limits apply at the AWS account level itself (for example, the maximum number of AWS accounts in an Organization). Understanding this distinction matters when you are troubleshooting: hitting an EC2 quota produces a different error than hitting an Organizations-level constraint.

The AWS Service Quotas console (service-quotas.console.aws.amazon.com) is the canonical place to view and manage all quotas. It lists every service, every quota name, the default value, your current applied value, and whether the quota is adjustable. It also shows your pending increase requests and their approval status.

# List all quotas for a specific service
aws service-quotas list-service-quotas \
    --service-code ec2 \
    --region us-east-1

# Get a specific quota by its quota code
aws service-quotas get-service-quota \
    --service-code ec2 \
    --quota-code L-1216C47A   # Running On-Demand Standard instances (vCPUs)

# List AWS default values (not your current applied value)
aws service-quotas list-aws-default-service-quotas \
    --service-code lambda \
    --region us-east-1

# Find service codes for a service name
aws service-quotas list-services --query 'Services[?contains(ServiceName, `Lambda`)]'

AWS Trusted Advisor (available in all accounts, with more detail on paid support tiers) has a 'Service Limits' check that flags quotas you are approaching. For accounts on Business or Enterprise Support, it proactively emails alerts when utilization crosses 80%. The Trusted Advisor API can also be polled programmatically to build custom alerting pipelines.
Navigate to the Service Quotas console and select the service (e.g., Amazon EC2)
Find the quota by name or quota code
Click 'Request quota increase' and enter the desired value
Submit — many quotas are auto-approved instantly; others open a Support case

# Request a quota increase
aws service-quotas request-service-quota-increase \
    --service-code ec2 \
    --quota-code L-1216C47A \
    --desired-value 500 \
    --region us-east-1

# Check the status of your request
aws service-quotas get-requested-service-quota-change \
    --request-id a1b2c3d4-1234-5678-abcd-example12345

# List all open increase requests
aws service-quotas list-requested-changes-by-service \
    --service-code ec2 \
    --status PENDING

Timing tip: request quota increases at least 5 business days before a planned launch. 'Auto-approved' quotas apply in minutes; quotas that require AWS review can take 1–3 business days, and occasionally longer for very large increases or specialized instance types.
Most commonly hit; request per instance family
Shared across all functions in region
Includes read replicas and Multi-AZ standbys
Adjustable; hit quickly in multi-env setups
Each ALB counts regardless of listeners
Public certificates per account per year
Adjustable; hit in large multi-service orgs
REST and HTTP APIs count separately
Rarely hit; images within a repo unlimited
Non-adjustable quotas are architectural ceilings. Hitting one requires a design change, not a support ticket. The most common non-adjustable limits teams encounter:
Split permissions across multiple policies; use managed policies
Use Lambda layers; move assets to S3 and load at runtime
Split long jobs into Step Functions state machines
Store payload in S3; send S3 reference in message
Decompose large items; store BLOBs in S3
Use multipart upload for objects over 100 MB
True maximum; split archives if larger

AWS Service Quotas publishes utilization metrics to CloudWatch for many (not all) quotas. The metric namespace is AWS/Usage and the metric name is ResourceCount. You can build alarms that fire when you approach a limit without manually polling the API.

import boto3

cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch')

# Create an alarm that fires at 80% of the Lambda concurrency quota
# Default quota is 1000 concurrent executions
cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm(
    AlarmName='lambda-concurrency-80pct',
    AlarmDescription='Lambda concurrent executions at 80% of quota',
    Namespace='AWS/Lambda',
    MetricName='ConcurrentExecutions',
    Dimensions=[],
    Statistic='Maximum',
    Period=60,
    EvaluationPeriods=2,
    Threshold=800,   # 80% of default 1000; adjust if you increased quota
    ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold',
    TreatMissingData='notBreaching',
    AlarmActions=[
        'arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:quota-alerts'
    ]
)

# Check utilization via the Service Quotas console metric
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
    --namespace AWS/Usage \
    --metric-name ResourceCount \
    --dimensions Name=Type,Value=Resource Name=Resource,Value=vCPU Name=Service,Value=EC2 Name=Class,Value=Standard/OnDemand \
    --statistics Maximum \
    --period 3600 \
    --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
    --end-time 2024-01-02T00:00:00Z \
    --region us-east-1

import boto3

support = boto3.client('support', region_name='us-east-1')

# Get Trusted Advisor check results for Service Limits
response = support.describe_trusted_advisor_checks(language='en')
limit_check = next(
    c for c in response['checks']
    if c['name'] == 'Service Limits'
)

result = support.describe_trusted_advisor_check_result(
    checkId=limit_check['id'],
    language='en'
)

for resource in result['result']['flaggedResources']:
    if resource['status'] in ('warning', 'error'):
        region, service, limit_name, _, _, usage, limit = resource['metadata']
        print(f"{service} | {limit_name} | {region}: {usage}/{limit}")
For any significant new workload, complete this quota review before the first deployment:
Identify every AWS service the workload uses (Lambda, RDS, ALB, ECR, API Gateway, etc.)
For each service, list the quotas relevant to your expected scale (vCPUs, concurrent executions, DB instances)
Compare current applied quota vs projected peak demand with 2× headroom
Request increases for any quotas projected to exceed 50% of current applied value
Confirm increases are applied before launch day — never submit on launch day
Quota Increase Lead Times by Service
Up to 1 week; justify with use case
In a multi-account AWS Organization, every account has its own independent set of quotas. There is no organization-level pool that members share. This has several practical consequences:
A new account created via AWS Control Tower or Account Factory starts with default quotas — you must request increases in every account independently
Account Vending Machine automation should include a quota-increase step as part of account bootstrap
If you use separate accounts per environment (dev/staging/prod), production accounts often need higher quotas than dev accounts; request proportionally
Service Control Policies (SCPs) can restrict which services are usable per account, but cannot lower a quota below its applied value
Automating Quota Requests at Account Creation

import boto3
import time

def bootstrap_account_quotas(account_id: str, role_name: str):
    """Request standard quota increases for a newly vended account."""
    sts = boto3.client('sts')
    creds = sts.assume_role(
        RoleArn=f'arn:aws:iam::{account_id}:role/{role_name}',
        RoleSessionName='QuotaBootstrap'
    )['Credentials']

    sq = boto3.client(
        'service-quotas',
        region_name='us-east-1',
        aws_access_key_id=creds['AccessKeyId'],
        aws_secret_access_key=creds['SecretAccessKey'],
        aws_session_token=creds['SessionToken'],
    )

    increases = [
        ('ec2',    'L-1216C47A', 500),   # vCPUs Standard
        ('lambda', 'L-B99A9384', 3000),  # Concurrent executions
        ('vpc',    'L-F678F1CE', 50),    # VPCs per region
        ('elasticloadbalancing', 'L-53DA6B97', 100),  # ALBs per region
    ]

    for service_code, quota_code, desired in increases:
        try:
            sq.request_service_quota_increase(
                serviceCode=service_code,
                quotaCode=quota_code,
                desiredValue=desired,
            )
            print(f"Requested {service_code}/{quota_code} -> {desired}")
        except sq.exceptions.ResourceAlreadyExistsException:
            print(f"Request already open for {service_code}/{quota_code}")
        time.sleep(0.5)  # avoid throttling
Quota codes are stable identifiers you can use in scripts, IaC, and automation without relying on human-readable quota names that occasionally change:
Running On-Demand G and VT instances

Quota errors and throttling errors look similar in CloudWatch logs and SDK exceptions, but they have different root causes and different remediation paths. Confusing them leads to spending time on the wrong fix.
API call rate too high right now
Implement exponential backoff and jitter; reduce call frequency
You have reached a count quota
Request a quota increase or delete unused resources
Request EC2 vCPU limit increase for the instance family
Request Lambda concurrency increase or add reserved concurrency
Clean up unused roles/users or request IAM limit increase

Well-written application code distinguishes between transient throttling (retry with backoff) and quota exhaustion (alert and fail fast). Retrying on quota exhaustion wastes resources and delays the alert that a human needs to act on.
Quota Management in Infrastructure as Code

CloudFormation does not have a built-in resource type for Service Quotas increase requests. The recommended approach is to use a CloudFormation Custom Resource backed by a Lambda function that calls the Service Quotas API. This ensures quota increases are tracked in your IaC repository and applied consistently across environments.

AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: Request quota increases as part of account bootstrap

Resources:
  QuotaRequesterRole:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal: {Service: lambda.amazonaws.com}
            Action: sts:AssumeRole
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: ServiceQuotasAccess
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - servicequotas:RequestServiceQuotaIncrease
                  - servicequotas:GetRequestedServiceQuotaChange
                Resource: '*'

  EC2VCpuQuota:
    Type: Custom::ServiceQuotaIncrease
    Properties:
      ServiceToken: !GetAtt QuotaRequesterFunction.Arn
      ServiceCode: ec2
      QuotaCode: L-1216C47A
      DesiredValue: 500

The AWS Terraform provider includes the aws_servicequotas_service_quota resource, which reads or requests quota values declaratively. Note that Terraform can only request increases — it cannot force-set quotas, and the resource will show a 'planned change' any time the quota value drifts (e.g., AWS revises a default).

Terraform state tip: The aws_servicequotas_service_quota resource in Terraform state reflects the requested value, not necessarily the approved value. If an increase is pending review, terraform plan will continue to show a difference until AWS approves and applies it. Check the Service Quotas console for the actual applied value.

Production accounts accumulate quota increase requests over time, often submitted by different engineers for different workloads. Keeping a quota registry — even a simple spreadsheet or a JSON file in your IaC repository — prevents the confusion of 'why is our Lambda limit 8,000? who requested that?' and gives the team a baseline for planning future increases.

AWS requires a business justification for quota increase requests that undergo manual review. A vague justification ('we need more capacity') leads to back-and-forth with AWS Support and slower approvals. A well-structured justification gets approved faster and sets a precedent for future requests.

# Good quota increase request justification (paste into request form):

Service: Amazon EC2
Quota: Running On-Demand Standard (A,C,D,H,I,M,R,T,Z) instances
Current limit: 32 vCPUs
Requested limit: 500 vCPUs
Region: us-east-1

Business context:
We are migrating a production e-commerce workload from on-premises to AWS.
Peak load requires 120 m5.4xlarge instances (16 vCPUs each = 1,920 vCPU peak),
but we need 500 vCPUs as an initial step for the migration dry-run in Q3 2024.
We plan to submit a follow-on request for 2,000 vCPUs before go-live.

Current use case:
- 25 running m5.xlarge EC2 instances (4 vCPUs each = 100 vCPUs used)
- Burst to 500 during load tests over next 30 days

We accept that the increase is granted for us-east-1 only.

Key elements of an effective justification: the specific workload it supports, the timeline, the expected peak usage (show your math), and whether this is a one-time burst or permanent need. AWS Support uses this to verify the request is legitimate and to right-size the approval.

AWS service quotas are per-(account, region) limits that protect both AWS infrastructure and your workloads from runaway resource creation. Adjustable quotas can be raised via a support request; non-adjustable quotas require design changes to work around. The Service Quotas console and CLI are the primary tools for discovering current limits, submitting increase requests, and checking request status.

Proactive quota management means identifying relevant quotas before launch, requesting increases with 2× headroom and adequate lead time, and setting CloudWatch alarms at the 80% utilization threshold. In multi-account environments, quota increases must be requested per account per region — they never transfer automatically. Embedding quota bootstrapping into your account vending process ensures new accounts are production-ready from day one.
